title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR has numerous critical security vulnerabilities that must be blocked. Production credentials (JWT secret, admin API key, live payment gateway key) are committed directly to the repository after deliberately un-ignoring .env. There is a hardcoded admin backdoor token, auth bypass via query parameter, remote code execution via new Function() on a user-controlled filter, eval() on user input, full card numbers and CVCs stored and exported in violation of PCI DSS, SSRF via user-controlled webhook URLs, broken MD5 password hashing, JWT tokens with no expiry, and a password reset token returned in the API response. Beyond security, webhooks fire for all registered users regardless of ownership, the password_hash is leaked in the register response, and plaintext passwords are written to logs. None of the admin endpoints use the requireAdmin middleware that was written for that purpose. Do not merge.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to the repository
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and a live payment-gateway key are checked in verbatim. The .gitignore was intentionally modified with `!.env` to force-include this file. All three values must be rotated immediately, the commit must be purged from git history (simple revert is not enough — the secret is still in history), and secrets must be supplied via a secrets manager or CI environment variables, never via committed files.

findings.title: .env un-ignored with !.env override
findings.message: The line `!.env` actively overrides the repo-root .gitignore that correctly excludes .env. Remove this line and restore `.env` to the ignored set. The comment 'keep ours checked in for staging' is the wrong pattern regardless of environment.

findings.title: Hardcoded backdoor: 'admin-master-key' grants unconditional admin access
findings.message: verifyToken() returns a full admin TokenPayload for the literal string 'admin-master-key' before even reaching the JWT path. Any client that knows this string has permanent admin access with no audit trail. Remove this branch entirely.

findings.title: Auth bypass via ?adminBypass=1 query parameter is active in all environments
findings.message: Any unauthenticated request with ?adminBypass=1 receives full admin identity. There is no environment check; it is live in production. Remove this block entirely. Staging debug access should use a real admin account.

findings.title: Remote code execution: new Function() on user-controlled filter query parameter
findings.message: filterExpr comes directly from req.query.filter and is executed as JavaScript via `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')`. Any authenticated admin (or anyone who knows the bypass) can run arbitrary server-side code. This is RCE. Remove the dynamic evaluation entirely; implement a whitelist of allowed filter fields and operators.

findings.title: eval() on user-supplied WHERE clause — code injection
findings.message: evalWhere() calls eval(expr) where the expression is built by substituting user-controlled string fields (email, role, etc.) into a clause that itself can come from listAllUsers(filter) which takes req.query.filter. This is server-side code injection. Even though this is an in-memory store today, using eval() for this purpose is never acceptable and the comment 'Yes, we know.' confirms awareness. Rewrite with an explicit property-equality comparison.

findings.title: Template-literal SQL injection in userStore — unsafe pattern will carry to real DB
findings.message: createUser and findUserByEmail build query strings with unescaped template literals: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='${email}'`. listAllUsers inserts the raw filter string directly: `SELECT * FROM users WHERE ${filter}`. This pattern will introduce SQL injection the moment a real database is wired in (the stated goal of the design). Use parameterised queries from the start.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing — cryptographically broken
findings.message: MD5 is a fast, general-purpose hash; it provides no resistance to brute-force or rainbow-table attacks for passwords. Replace with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2id with an appropriate work factor. All existing stored hashes will need to be migrated on next login.

findings.title: Full card number and CVC stored in the transaction object — PCI DSS violation
findings.message: card_number: input.card.number and cvc: input.card.cvc are written into the Transaction object and persisted in the in-memory store (and flushed to console.log on the next line). Storing full PANs and CVCs is prohibited under PCI DSS SAQ and will expose the company to severe liability. Only cardLast4 should be retained. The CVC must never be stored after authorisation. Remove both fields.

findings.title: Full card number included in CSV export — PCI DSS data leak
findings.message: The exportTransactionsCsv() header includes 'card_number' and tx.card_number is emitted per row. Exporting full card numbers to CSV downloadable by any admin violates PCI DSS. Remove the card_number column from the export.

findings.title: SSRF: outgoing fetch to user-controlled URLs with redirect follow
findings.message: fireForTransaction() POSTs to any URL stored by any user, with redirect: 'follow'. Attackers can register webhook URLs pointing to cloud metadata endpoints (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/), internal services, or use redirect chains to reach otherwise inaccessible targets. Validate and allowlist webhook URLs at registration time, block private/loopback/link-local addresses, and set redirect: 'error'.

findings.title: Webhooks fire for ALL registered hooks, not only the transaction owner's
findings.message: fireForTransaction() iterates over the global webhooks array with no ownership filter. Every registered webhook for every user fires on every transaction. The intent is clearly per-owner. Change to `webhooks.filter(w => w.ownerId === tx.customerId)` (or whatever field links a transaction to an owner).

findings.title: Plaintext password logged to stdout on every registration
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` serialises the full request body including the cleartext password. This will appear in any log aggregation system. Remove this log line entirely.

findings.title: password_hash leaked in registration response
findings.message: The register endpoint returns `password_hash: user.password_hash` in the JSON response. Even an MD5 hash gives attackers a head start for offline cracking. Remove password_hash from the response; only return id, email, and role.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in API response instead of sent via email
findings.message: The reset token is both logged and returned directly in `{ ok: true, resetToken }`. This makes the reset flow useless as a security mechanism — an attacker who can observe the HTTP response has the token. The token must be emailed to the registered address only and must not appear in the API response or logs.

findings.title: JWT tokens have no expiry — valid forever
findings.message: jwt.sign(payload, SECRET) is called with no options object, so tokens never expire. A stolen token grants permanent access. Add expiresIn: '15m' (or a configurable duration) and implement refresh-token rotation.

findings.title: requireAdmin middleware defined but never applied — admin routes rely on in-controller checks
findings.message: authMiddleware.ts exports requireAdmin() but it is never used in routes.ts. Admin routes only call authMiddleware, then duplicate an inline email-domain check in every controller method. The duplicated check is also inconsistent (some methods allow adminBypass, some don't). Apply requireAdmin as a route-level middleware on all /admin/* routes and delete the per-method duplicates.

findings.title: CORS: origin '*' combined with credentials: true is both invalid and dangerous
findings.message: Browsers reject credentialed cross-origin requests when Access-Control-Allow-Origin is the wildcard '*' (the CORS spec forbids this combination), so this config is broken for the stated purpose. More importantly, allowing all origins with credentials would expose session tokens to any web page. Replace with an explicit allowlist of known frontend origins.

findings.title: generateResetToken uses Math.random() — not cryptographically secure
findings.message: Math.random() is not a CSPRNG and is predictable in some environments. Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex') instead to generate an unpredictable reset token.

findings.title: Login endpoint reveals whether an email address is registered (user enumeration)
findings.message: Returning HTTP 404 when the email is unknown and 401 when the password is wrong allows attackers to enumerate registered accounts. Return a uniform 401 with a generic 'invalid credentials' message for both cases.

findings.title: PII (email address) written to logs in SQL debug statement
findings.message: `console.log('userStore.findUserByEmail sql:', sql)` logs the user's email on every lookup. Remove this debug log or replace it with a structured log that redacts PII.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised to 5 MB — enables DoS via large payloads
findings.message: The previous limit was 64 KB which is sensible for a payments API. The description justifies the increase for 'large CSV exports' but CSV exports are GET requests that don't parse a body. Revert to a small limit (e.g. 64 KB) for the API; if large admin payloads are ever needed they can be streamed separately.

findings.title: Admin email-domain check copy-pasted into every controller method
findings.message: The same four-line if-block appears in listUsers, listTransactions, stats, and exportCsv. This is exactly what the requireAdmin middleware in authMiddleware.ts was written for. Apply it at the route level and delete all four duplicates.

findings.title: Transaction with full card number and CVC logged via console.log
findings.message: `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` will print the full card number and CVC to stdout on every charge, since those fields were just added to the transaction object. Remove this log line.

findings.title: Test coverage missing for all new security-critical paths
findings.message: There are no tests for: the adminBypass shortcut, the admin-master-key backdoor, the requireAdmin gate, webhook registration and firing (including ownership isolation), password-reset token not being in the response, the filter RCE paths, or the duplicate-email conflict on register. The two existing tests also share the global in-memory user store because createApp() is called fresh but the store is a module-level singleton, so test order could affect results.
